topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an unnerving intensity. Sarah clutched the binder to her chest, the edges already digging into her palms. Mr. Henderson, his face a mask of practiced calm, gestured toward the empty chair opposite her. "So, Sarah," he began, his voice smooth as polished stone, "you'll be guiding Mark through the onboarding process." A cold knot tightened in Sarah's stomach. She swallowed hard, the dryness in her throat making the action difficult. *This is a precipice,* she thought, watching the dust motes dance in the harsh light. Every muscle in her jaw felt tight. She rehearsed opening lines in her head, but the words seemed to dissolve into a thick, ungraspable mist. When Mark finally walked in, radiating an easy confidence that felt like a physical weight pressing down on Sarah, she could only manage a weak nod, her gaze darting everywhere but his eyes.

[story 2]

I kept smoothing the fabric of my blouse, a nervous tic I hadn't realized I possessed. The air in the conference room felt thin, charged with unspoken expectation. When the manager announced the assignment—training the new hire—my breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did nothing to settle the tremor running through my hands. I looked down at my shoes, focusing intently on the scuff marks, anything to avoid meeting anyone's gaze. The thought of having to articulate complex procedures, of being the sole anchor for someone new, sent a wave of nausea washing over me. "I... I can try," I managed, the sound barely above a whisper, my voice cracking on the final syllable. I felt a desperate urge to become invisible, to simply melt into the beige wall behind me.

[story 3]

The instructions felt like a labyrinth designed to trap me. I reread the training manual for the tenth time, the glossy paper blurring under my anxious scrutiny. When the supervisor laid out the expectations for the transition, my vision tunneled. My palms began to sweat, slick and cold against the laminated pages. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the supply closet, but my feet felt cemented to the linoleum floor. Every syllable I prepared felt clumsy and inadequate. Looking at the expectant faces of the team, I could only stare at my own reflection in the darkened monitor screen—a pale, shaky image of someone utterly unprepared for the spotlight. The silence stretched, heavy and suffocating, amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest.
